text: Maps compress the world into something holdable. A continent fits in your palm; a city becomes a grid you can trace with one finger. They are tools of orientation first — they answer where am I, which way, how far. But they also reveal patterns invisible at ground level: watershed boundaries, trade routes, the geometry of sprawl. Every map involves choices about what to include and what to omit, which means every map has a point of view. The London Tube map famously distorts geography to prioritize clarity of use. That trade-off is honest. A map that tried to show everything would show nothing. Knowing what to leave out is the cartographer's core skill, and maybe a useful principle beyond mapmaking.
